title: vimeo_1204066212
displayTitle: 2026-06-25 - Social Services and Community Committee - Social Services and Community Sub-committee A
topic: Social Services and Community Sub-committee A
description: Thursday, 25th June 2026 | Social Services and Community Sub-committee A Disability Support Services Bill Hearing of evidence: Katie Townsend Rebkah Corlett Seinna Carrington Claire Vincent Tania Pointon Foundation for Equity and Research New Zealand (FERNZ) Joseph Boon David Clarke Simon Cossey Suzanne Phibbs David Chalk Red Nicholson Luka Willems Ronni Mudgway Pip Brunn (lucy brunn) Thea King Le-Anne Tatana Sharon Cooke
descriptionSummary: Social Services and Community Sub-committee A. Disability Support Services Bill. Katie Townsend

chunks.text: Katie, is it? Yes. Welcome. We're just going to do a little introduction quickly just for everyone watching. Yeah. We'll kick on, get underway. Okay. All right. Good afternoon, everyone. We are back with the Social Services and Community Select Committee, and we've got two sub-committees today. So I'm chairing one sub-committee.

And so I'm Joseph Mundy, the Member of Parliament for Southland and the chair of the committee. And we'll just go quickly around who else is here. Hi, I'm Laura McClure. I'm the Act list MP based down in Christchurch. Kia ora. I'm Helen White. I'm the MP for Mount Albert. I've also got a couple of portfolios, the Family and Sexual Violence Prevention portfolio and Community and Voluntary.

I'm going to be replaced at some point in this committee by Priyanka Ramakrishnan, who has the Disability portfolio. Kia ora. My name is Ricardo Menéndez March. I'm a Green Party MP based in Mount Albert, and I do Social Development and Employment, as well as Immigration and a few other portfolios for the Greens.

Welcome, Katie. Thanks for having you this afternoon. Thanks for coming in to make a submission. The time is yours when you're ready. Thank you. And thanks for the opportunity. So to introduce myself, I'm Katie Townsend. I'm currently a student at Victoria University of Wellington, doing political science and public policy.

And disability is an interest of mine because I am disabled myself and have several family friends in Wellington and Christchurch. Great. Thanks. So the first concern is regarding Clause 8 and the family first. So, it brings the assumption that families have the capability and resources to be able to provide care
for the disabled member, which is not always the case. And
also, in a lot of cases, it is unpaid. Mm-hmm. And those who do get paid, it is usually because they're eligible for the Supportive Living Payment.

So, some of these considerations before providing care, first of all is willingness. I do understand that family does have the natural responsibility to provide care, but that may not always be the case. It could be because they may not have the financial resources available, or they may not have the skills, especially in cases where the disabled person has complex needs.

And also other commitments, such as other family members, and also work and, in some cases, say, the family member may live in another part of the country or the world. So, my recommendation is, one would be a carer capacity assessment to make sure that they do have that capacity to provide that quality care.

My second concern is regarding Clause 11, and how the majority of that framework is passed through secondary legislation, which, from my understanding, does not go under the same public debate and scrutiny as primary legislation. But it's also, you have to rely, because you have to decide what-- had to put trust on that minister

to serve in the community's best interests, and without any safeguards in place- Yeah those decisions can be made based on government priorities instead of the interest in the community. The third one is consultation. Yes, there may be some consultation, but for a framework, it probably would need to be a lot more comprehensive to make sure, especially the ministers and the people creating that bill,

understand those real-world impacts, and make sure that the objectives of the disability community and the government align with each other. And the final concern is with the bill itself. First of all, it relies, that's from my own analysis, it's quite heavily focused on family circumstances instead of the support needs of the person.

And another one is, it's quite looked at from a... If we're talking about disability support services, it focuses on care support. And despite when disability support services provide a lot of other things, such as housing, transport, child development services that I have used previously, education, and employment services.

And I do believe if we're doing a framework, it has to cover everything, especially in the primary legislation. And the last one, it's quite focused on from a social development point of view. When you actually-- Because it talks a little bit more about funding allocation- Mm... resource allocation, but disability affects a lot of different things, not just social development. It affects health, education, employment, housing and transport, and other sectors.

So, we do need to have a-- It shouldn't be based on a single department. It should be more of a cross-agency approach to make sure it covers all areas. Great. Thanks, Cody. I'd love to ask you questions, but we don't have time. But thank you very much- Yeah. It's all good... for your presentation this afternoon. Appreciate it.
Thank you. Thank you very much.

Got it. Thank you. All right, next submitter is Rebecca, I think also in person. Welcome. Kia ora. Kia ora. All right. We've got five minutes with you this evening as well, so time is yours-... when you are ready. Cool. Thank you. Kia ora. My name is Rebecca Collett.

I'm here as a parent of a teenager with high needs who lives at home. I'm not very good with eye contact, however, it's important for me to be here today to look you- No, thank you... in the eye. To say this: please don't proceed with this bill. Don't find yourselves on the wrong side of history.

This bill will negatively impact yet another generation of disabled people and their families in Aotearoa. The fact that this bill has made it to this stage shows that we have learnt nothing of how to treat disabled people from the Royal Commission of Inquiry into Abuse and State Care.

It's a spit on the unmarked graves of disabled people that lived and died in institutions, and it's the ultimate betrayal, I think, to the survivors who gave evidence in good faith and trusted in the process, hopeful that it would help shape a better future for future generations of disabled people.

We said never again, yet here we are disregarding disabled people's human rights. Institutions in the past hid people away, denying access to education, healthcare, employment, community, and it feels like this is happening again. It's same but different. This time it's happening in slow motion and in full view of all of us.

Institutions of the past were four walls made of brick and mortar. People were placed far away from family and community. Now, with supports being stripped back, disabled people are effectively being controlled and isolated in their own homes with no free will as to who cares for them, what services they engage with, and how they live their lives.

I today heard the Minister of Disability saying that this bill will not change day-to-day life for disabled people and their families. Funding will stay the same and new means testing will not be introduced. However, my opinion is that this bill sets the rules for the future. So it might not change today, but what about tomorrow?

Making it easier to limit access, cap support, and remove ability for recourse when the wheels fall off. The current government might have the ability to stop the rollout of enabling good lives, to lessen subsidies for total mobility scheme, to tinker with the purchasing guidelines of individualized funding, but the UNCRPD still exists, and the government has an obligation to support disabled people to live their lives without barriers.

And families, like my own, who have long plugged up the gaps in an unsupportive system, forsaking their own physical and mental wellbeing, will now be expected to financially support their disabled loved ones on top of everything else, no matter what their capacity is.

This government continues to punch down on vulnerable communities to save a buck. Of all the changes that they can make to disabled people's lives, how is this the one that they chose to make? The snowball effect this bill will have for disabled people and their families will be immense.

For me, if we can't afford to pay a carer for my daughter, I'll have to give up my own employment, which means I can't provide for my family, which will detrimentally impact my own self-worth and mental health, which means I can't be the mum I want to be to my kids. This bill should not proceed, and if it must, you should at least extend the hearing process so you can hear from more people around the country who will be impacted by this bill. 19 days for submissions and five minutes per oral submission for a select

few is negligent, and this is very anxiety-- It's given me anxiety, but you have an obligation to listen to as many of the very people that this bill will impact. So committee members, please do the right thing, not the expected thing. Ka mua, ka muri.

Let the past guide how we move forward. Thank you. Thank you. Ooh. Um Made it under five. Well done. Nice. Well done. Rebecca. Just a really quick question. Thank you. What do you see could be some of the worsening outcomes for disabled people under the bill as it stands?

I really am concerned about health. Mental health. I'm concerned about carers who are already at their limit that are going to have to take on more responsibilities, and this will detrimentally impact our mental health across the board. Sure. Thank you.

People are already struggling, and looking for support in the wrong places. So, yeah, that's my concern. Thank you. Thank you. Oh, thanks, Rebecca. Thanks for coming in and making submissions. I think. Thank you. Our next submitter is Sienna via Zoom.

Can you hear me? We can, yes. Thank you. Yes. Oh, welcome, Sienna. So you have five minutes this afternoon. The time is yours when you're ready. Oh, thank you. Tēnā koutou katoa. Thank you for giving me the opportunity to speak today. I would like to acknowledge the people that have spoke before me. My name is Sienna, and I'm a mother of a 26-year-old daughter, with significant disabilities and complex medical needs My daughter lives at home and needs full-time support every day. For the last 26 years, our family has organized our

lives around her care. With one parent have always stayed at home and the other went to work. As a result, we have lived on one income for the past 26 years. This has meant major sacrifices in income, career opportunities, retirement saving, financial security, along with just the daily emotional and physical demand of care.

And those challenges are shared by many New Zealand families across, that provide support for their family members with disabilities. We do have a duty of care as families, but the government also has the responsibility to support their citizens with disabilities, and uphold their mana.

This bill is trembling on everybody's mana. I go to work every day. I don't ask for a lot, but this bill is just... It's awful. Family support should not be substituted for government support. As a citizen, people with disabilities should be supported.

My biggest concern about the Disability Support Service Bill, it seems to be focused more on controls, reassessment, management of financial, instead of the well-being of the people that it's meant to be providing for. I'm concerned that the assessment and reassessment will create even more uncertainties, especially for the people with special needs and their families.

My daughter's special needs are well-documented, so a new assessment is not going to change what had happened for the last 26 years. It's just going to create an environment of distrust, and it's going to create anxiety and insecurity, and it just impacts the well-being of our whanau. What I would like to see is a system that is built with working with us as families with the disability support services, that can actually look at the real picture and the impact on families and people with special needs. Not what has happened

today. This bill was put together so the government can save money, and it has not taken our needs. It's not listened to us, and it is true. The consultation, five minutes for each speaker. You are dealing with a group of people that are already tired.

We already give a lot. Our children don't grow up and leave and have their own lives and their own careers. I don't come home from work and don't have anything to do. I have another job that I come home to, and that's what it's been like for 26 years.

We want a greater recognition of the family carers and the contribution that we actually put in. The fact is that we have to fight for everything. It's dehumanizing. It just makes you feel like you're the worst parent, even though you really do try as a parent. I go and work full time, and I come home and I work full time.

And this bill really-- It's awful. At the heart of it, the bill should ensure that people with disability can live with dignity, security, choices, and fully participate in society. This is not what this bill going to create. We're not asking for special treatment.

We are asking for fairness, stability, and recognition. And the government has an ongoing responsibility for its citizens, regardless if they are able to work or not. My child is a citizen of this country. I pay my taxes. I'm not actually asking for a lot.

Just please don't change the system so much. Thank you. Thanks, Janice. Thanks for making submission. And look, I'll just make the comment that in my view, it's not doing that. But we don't have time to go on that. But can I just make one other comment, because you're the second person to say this, this afternoon, about five minutes. The committee's made a decision to give five minutes to every submitter.

There's a lot of people who want to make submissions, so we're trying to hear from as many as possible, and so it's quite normal for committees to give five minutes for submitters on bills. But- But thanks for making submissions this afternoon Can I just make a point here? Yeah, go for it. There is a limited brief on this. We have to have it back by the 13th of August, and that's a decision that's been taken by the minister. So we are in a position that is truncated.

I know- Oh, but Helen, we as a committee, we decide how much time we set aside- We couldn't possibly- And we're doing it... we couldn't possibly- We could... sit out- We're doing it... because we have to have it back because of what the government- Helen, we could sit three weekends if we wanted to. So- It's really important submitters know why we are doing this in such a rushed fashion.

It's normal, Helen, for committees to do five-minute hearings. Mr. Chair, could you hand the time, including your time, back to submitters? Because I don't think this is our time to bicker. It's their time. It was not bickering, Ricardo. I mean, you're literally bickering with presenters. I think it's been balanced. I think it's been, but yeah All right. Now, I'm sorry, Luciana, that was not about you.

That was a discussion between committee members with a bit of a political difference on things. But thanks for making a submission this afternoon. We appreciate it. Thank you. Our next submitter is Claire. Madam President.

Good afternoon. Welcome. Thanks, Claire. All right. We've got five minutes with you when you're ready. Fire away. Cool. Thank you. I just wanted to focus on a few things. I know a lot of people have talked about the impact this has on people's lives. Obviously, I've got disabilities myself, so I'm a bit invested in this, but I am also somebody who implements a lot of legislation, and I'm quite concerned with this particular piece.

Mm-hmm. The key problems is that it appears to be yet another panic piece, where it's solely aimed at shutting down a specific group of society seeking a remedy that has been breached in some way, whether it be environmental or whether it be offshore and seabed with-- and now we're looking at employment law based on human rights.

It's ill-considered, and the result of this would be a declaration of inconsistency coming through, because it is breaching-- Well, breaching, but it is inconsistent with both our ratification of the UNCRDP and also the Human Rights Act. And I do also have concerns over the way it was formed when you consider things like Section 7.68 of the Cabinet Manual, Section 5 of the Standing Orders, which

suggest that whoever creates a bill should be considering things like the Bill of Rights and really consider good reasons as to why you would create a conflict with that. And right now, the only real conflict is we have no plan-- the justification, sorry, is that we have no plan, and we think it's going to cost too much.

So we're going to basically remove our accountability to the New Zealand public by creating this conflict in the Act. It is also worrying because it childifies people with disabilities. A child is the responsibility of their family until they become an adult.

And a child, if they can't be cared for, their family then becomes the responsibility of the state to step in. Excuse me. That is essentially what you're doing with people with disabilities. You are saying that you will only step in as the state if the family can't support them, and that puts them on a similar level as a child.

It also impacts everybody, regardless of disability, because this would look at people with age, their working history, and all other parts of society. It's quite a scattergun piece. What I think should happen is that we kill this bill before the election. There are some reasons for that.

And it bases around the fact that we really need a good trunk of legislation. A really good piece of legislation is something that it can endure multiple parties, multiple terms, multiple philosophies because those principles are really solid. If you think of the Employment Relations Act that came in in 2000, it's got a good trunk. You've trimmed around the outside now and again, but those trunk of principles have remained, and that's what I would like to see, this bill be changed to include these really good principles.

I'd also recommend that we look at things from a horizontal viewpoint rather than silos. So we've got a disability strategy at the moment, but it's all about health. We haven't looked at things like the intersectionality between health, education, social services, justice, infrastructure and transport, New Zealand Emergency Management and Civil Defense, employment, and other things. It's a horizontal way of looking at things, and it goes across the silos. We've got a lot of strategy and decisions over a silo looking at health

or looking at the ERA reports in education, or we're looking at social services. We've not got that nice horizontal look in how everybody interacts along the way, and that's where some of this conflict is coming in. So that's where I would like to see us to start to resolve that. I can see I've got 10 seconds left. Sorry.

No, thank you. Just on that last point, actually, because I think these people wanting sort of an integrated approach, and I understand that, but this is about disability support services, which is not education, it's not housing. It's not these other services which have their own discrete funding pots.

They do, but they create conflict and silos when you look at only disability support services, but you're not looking at how they're getting to school. Or you're not looking at how this impacts the entire family who are now out of the workforce, for example, and needing to seek benefits because they're taking care of somebody.

And this interaction all the way across actually has quite a wide Impact on a lot of different people. So it is worth checking that to look for discrepancies, look at funding holes, and you're not going to get that view unless you do those horizontal checks.

It's just a matrix way of doing policy and legislation checks to make sure that you are being consistent and you are covering those holes so that you can make really good, informed decisions, whether it be at budget time or whether or not it's your policy decision.

If you're not doing those horizontal checks, you're not looking for the holes and figuring out how to plan to resolve them. And that's what- We're well over time. So I've got the point now. Thank you. Appreciate you coming to make submissions this afternoon. Yes, thank you. All right, our next submitter is Tania in person.
I think we've got a couple of us preparing. Was I right?
Good afternoon. Which one is Tania? Ah. I go up and tell you. Welcome. We have five minutes with you this afternoon. The time is yours when you're ready.

Kia ora koutou. I'm here today as a friend and ally of all our disabilities communities in Aotearoa. I'm joined by my friend and colleague Juliana and Vera. It's ironic that I'm the one that gets to speak at this process because the communities that are affected the most by this bill have been silenced in this very undemocratic process.

It's really quite shameful. I was just listening and I'm off script now, but the speed at which this has been done reminds me of a quote that we often use in the community. This is important, so slow down. Let's really think about that. What's the point of rushing this?

And the other thing I have to wonder about is why are so many coming to submit. Something must be wrong. No matter what Minister Upston tries to tell us or to make us believe, this bill will change things. It opens up the loopholes. So to make things really simple and clear for you, we're going to do a little role play.

Vera is representing Juliana's family system. Vera has an already full life. Vera has work, whanau commitments, bills to pay, cleaning and cooking, and let's try and squeeze in a bit of social life. Along comes this bill, shifting the burden. All the things that Juliana needs, transport, getting to medical appointments.

Enough. Enough. Enough. This bill rips away our rights. This bill, you know it's going to do? It's going to destroy my family quality of life. This bill is going to destroy as well my independence. And this bill is going to destroy my dignity.

And I'm not being alarmist here, but when I commit suicide because this bill will make my life so miserable that that's the only option that I have left, the blood is on your hands. And when desperate moms who are exhausted to the bone kill their disabled children because they can no longer take it, is their blood in your hands?

But the story doesn't end here. And there is one thing that this bill cannot take away from us, and that's our hope. And I have a message for everyone watching. History teaches that rights are never handed down by those with power to those who are oppressed.

Rights are claimed. Women in Aotearoa organized and claimed their rights to vote. Black communities America organized and claimed their civil rights. And right now in New Zealand, we are organizing, and you're giving us a very good push to do that. We are organizing. We are coming together. We are building power.

And I invite you all listening to this oral submission, visit the DPA website, learn about finding common ground. Together the National Leadership for Enabling Good Lives, Te Ao Marama Aotearoa, Disabled Persons Assembly, we are inviting all the community to come together and shape a declaration for disability rights in Aotearoa. This is way more than a document.

This is a tool for us to organize, to unite, and to claim our rights for us by us. Nothing about us without us. Honestly, let's do the right thing. Kill the bill. And the other thing is your maths are so wrong. You really think you're saving money?

This narrative is so harmful. And finally, my last message is they're trying to make us powerless, but we can vote. So make sure you enroll, and let's crip the vote. Thank you so much. All right. Our next submitter, Foundation for Equity and Research New Zealand in person.
Thank you. Thank you.

Oh, okay. Cool. Your family. Oh. I think we just want to have another camera. Right. Good afternoon and welcome. Good afternoon. Pam, Dr Pam, we've got you online. Can you just make sure you can hear us okay? Yes. Absolutely. Now, just your camera, if you wanted us to see your face, we can't quite see it at the moment.

I'm blind, so you might want to give me a wee clue. Oh, yeah. Just tip the camera upwards a bit. Yeah, there we go. Perfect. Perfect. Thank you. Thank you. Thanks so much. All right. So look, we've got time with you this afternoon. So look, welcome and thanks for coming along and making a submission. Thank you.

Thank you. Kia ora. My name is Pip McLean. I hold the disability rights portfolio for the Foundation for Equity and Research New Zealand, or FERNZ for short. Joining me today on Zoom is Dr Pam McNeil, who is a member of our Hutt Valley Disability Focus Community Action Group, and sitting next to me is Mr Mark Drews, who is co-chair of our Kapiti Disability Community Action Group.

Today we're speaking on behalf of the FERNZ disability-focused Wellington Regional Community Action groups. We'd like to acknowledge that you've asked us to address three questions, and we're very happy to do that. Thank you. The first question you have asked is what changes would you like to see to the bill? Pam. The bill must be amended to align with enabling good lives principles and New Zealand's obligations under the United Nations Convention on Rights for Persons with Disabilities.

Mark. Specifically, pause the bill and undertake a genuine consultation and co-design with disabled people and remove Clause 8 and 11.3 so that support is not dependent on family availability and income or asset testing. The second question you've asked us is what is your biggest concern? Pam.

The bill moves disability support services away from a rights-based model to a system where decisions are made about us without us. Mark. No real consultation has been given. Unfair burdens on families and the financial risk and account taken of the costs of disability.

Pam. The overall concern is that the bill attempts to infantilize disabled people, removing our right to agency over our own lives. Pip. The third question you've asked is what would your solution be? Pam. Genuinely co-design, not merely consult with disabled people.

Ensure the legislation is grounded in enabling good lives principles and human rights obligations. Mark. The solution, sorry. This would include pausing the bill and redesigning a thorough, genuine co-design process led by disabled people, and ensuring support is based on disability-related

need, not family availability, income, savings, or assets. Pip. So in summary: pause the bill, remove Clauses 8 and 11.3, embed choice and control protections, and require genuine co-design and consultation with disabled people before any decisions affecting disability support are made. Thank you for listening to us.

Oh, thank you very much. I might just leap in with a question I had in my head first. I'm wondering, and I appreciate the consultation issues that have been raised. But for example, just around Section 8, it says, "Family support where appropriate." So obviously, there will need to be an assessment made about whether it's appropriate to rely on family support or not. I just haven't heard anyone address that point, because I think there's been an assumption that- I'd like to do that... support.

Sorry, what was that, Dr- Can I do that? So where appropriate, who's making the decision where appropriate? I think what happens with a lot of disabled people is that through our lives, decisions are made about us without us. Decisions are made over our lives. We have no agency, and we fight for that along with our colleagues and our peers as teenagers and young adults.

And then when we finally get that, we want to keep it. We don't want other people telling us what's appropriate for us. But unfortunately, that continues to happen. And whether your view on what's appropriate family support and my view are probably going to be diametrically opposed. Sure. All right. No, thanks for addressing that, Dr Pam. Appreciate it.
Look, thank you to all three of you. Appreciate you coming and making a submission this afternoon. Thank you for your time.
Thank you. Joseph. Thank you. We're up. Who's your friend?

Brian. Yeah. Yeah. Please ignore her. Oh, no. We'd love to see her. What's her name? If you want to share, you don't have to. I beg your pardon? Oh, just wondering what the name of your friend is. Brian. Brian. Oh, cheers. Well, lovely to have you here, Joseph.

Thanks for coming and making a submission this afternoon. The five minutes with you when you're ready. Thank you very much. Tēnā koutou katoa. Ko Joseph toku ingoa. I go by Joe. Thank you for allowing me time to speak on this very clement Wellington day.

For context, I was diagnosed, along with my brother and sister, with Friedreich's ataxia in 2008. Since I must be very brief, I'll simply say that it is a rare inherited genetic disorder that causes progressive damage to the nervous system.

From the start, I suggest that you either delete the references to secondary legislation or scrap this pretend legislative framework entirely. A true framework must clearly state our rightful supports rather than leaving the substance to unspecified regulations at the discretion of future ministers. You cannot have both a robust framework and the flexibility to chop and change on a whim.

This bill does the latter while pretending to be the former. In simple terms, don't urinate on my leg and call it rain. The minister admitted that she's moving quickly because of the Supreme Court case, which forced this bill off slower track.

I'm sure she knows, as you do, that passing retroactive legislation to spare the Crown's blushes and purse is dishonorable. Doing this for the fifth time this term erodes public respect for the rule of law, and perhaps that shame is why it has been tagged onto a bill that isn't ready.

I live with my sister in an accessible house owned by my parents. Whenever there is a gap in support, they fill it, despite imminent retirement and having both worked at the coalface of the health system for over 40 years. Yet our needs keep growing.

After this bill, are my parents to be further impoverished? Is their privacy to be infringed upon by arbitrary eyes? In 2011, the Honorable Tariana Turia listened to and understood the disability community. She established trust, which was strengthened by a degree of political consensus.

But when the Honorable Penny Simmonds abruptly issued half purchasing guidelines and Cabinet stripped DSS from Whakaha, that trust was shattered. No effort has since been made to rebuild that trust. Consequently, we do not believe the minister's assurances that supports will remain unchanged or that further means testing won't be introduced. Mr Chair, you frequently ask submitters if they are aware of the nearly

$3 billion committed to DSS by this government. Yes, sir, I am aware. It is a band-aid. You may not realize that DSS was chronically underfunded within the Ministry of Health, masking its deficits under a larger health budget, an option unavailable to Whakaha in 2024.

Instead of consulting the community for a better plan, the minister panicked, and here we are. Sir, you also told the Health and Disability Commissioner on Tuesday that because the invaluable role disabled people play in their families and communities is, as you say, axiomatic, incorporating that into law would probably make no difference. But isn't the principle that families have a duty to help their disabled kin also an axiom?

Why then should one be codified in this bill while the other left out? Honorable members, whatever you decide to do, and I hope you recommend this bill not proceed, your mahi matters. This process, expedited as it is, is vital. Representing all our people in a system is imperfect, as any evolving product of humanity really counts.

You lead lives of consequence, lives that really, truly matter. I hope we do too. Thank you very much, Joe. A very articulate submission. And thank you very much for coming and making it through this inclement weather to come and share that with us. And- I beg your pardon?

Thank you very much for coming and sharing your very articulate submission with us, and making it through the inclement weather, as you said. And I wish you all the best on the journey back with Bree. Thank you. Thank you very much. Kia ora.
And you very much do matter. Can I just say it as well?
Are we ready to go with the next one, please? Yeah. Are you ready to go? Yep. Cool. Great. All right, next submitter is David by Zoom.

Good evening, David. Welcome. Thank you. Kia ora koutou. Kia ora. We have five minutes with you when you're ready, far away. My name is David Clark. Thank you for the opportunity to speak today. I would like to give you some context for who this legislation affects.

My brother is severely disabled. He developed type 1 diabetes as a child and has been insulin-dependent for 50 years. In his early adulthood, he went deaf and has a cochlear implant. He subsequently developed rheumatoid arthritis before effective treatments existed, and he has had elbows and knees replaced, and his hands are twisted claws.

He struggles to hold utensils and cups. His mobility is very poor. Recently, he had one of his legs amputated above the knee because of poor circulation due to diabetes and an MRSA infection he acquired while in hospital. He is stoic and fiercely independent and trapped in a body that just doesn't work.

He is alive today because of the support and advocacy provided by our parents, who are now in their late 80s. Dad will be 89 this week. They still take him to appointments because his care provider doesn't have adequate staffing. They communicate with medical staff on his behalf because he is deaf, and hospital staff often are unaware that he cannot read their lips when they're wearing masks.

The current disability support system is not adequate and doesn't meet my brother's needs. My elderly parents are the informal supports for my brother. What happens when they are no longer able to provide support for him because of their age and their own health issues? And my parents' situation is by no means unique.

There are many parents of disabled children, young people, and adults very anxious about what happens to their child when they're no longer able to provide the necessary support, and many who struggle to provide that support currently. This DSS bill embeds language about natural supports and family responsibility that risks shifting more unpaid care onto families who are already stretched.

This family's first principle, and an unusually broad definition of who counts as family, casts an extremely wide net, including uncles and aunts, nephews and nieces. This framework looks less about how communities support each other, and more like a list of people the state can expect to step in before public funding is approved.

As Debbie Waters said, do we believe disabled children, young people, and adults have a right to essential support from the government? Right now, this bill answers that question through the back door by giving a future minister the power to introduce means testing by written notice. There are no safeguards in this legislation, no minimum floor, no parliamentary approval required.

This DSS bill locks in a charity-era view of disability, narrows people's choices, and removes existing rights without proper public input. Most of the detail of who is eligible, what supports are funded, how decisions are made, is left to secondary legislation.

Which means that future ministers can change the rules through regulation without the level of debate in public submissions that primary legislation requires. Why does the DSS bill seek to introduce broad powers to consider informal support, such as that provided by my parents, within the core disability support framework?

And what policy outcome is intended that cannot already be achieved under existing legislation? A legislative basis for DSS is needed to provide support for my brother and other vulnerable and disabled people, but not this insecure, poorly written legislation. Our disabled children, young people, adults, and their whanau deserve better.

Some of us want to be supported by our whānau, and when that happens, we expect them to be appropriately compensated. That is a tricky thing for our marriage to navigate, but it was our choice, and we make it work. consultation. The minister wants to make it clear that this bill, quote, "Doesn't change anything for any disabled person today." I'm a disabled Maori woman living with a lifelong impacts of severe childhood burn injuries. When I was 18 months old, both my feet were severely burnt in a bath of hot water.

As a result, I eventually lost nine toes. Although my support mainly comes through ACC, I know that the disability policy affects all disabled people. It affects our whanau, our communities, and the generations that are yet to come. Even after almost 50 years, I am still living with the consequences of these injuries.

My biggest concern about the bill is the direction it may take. To me, it feels like disabled people will have to keep proving themselves over and over again to get the support we need. Living with disability is already exhausting. We live with pain, fatigue, surgeries, appointments, and barriers that many people never see. We shouldn't also have to fight the very system that is supposed to support us.

For me, disability isn't just about my feet. It affects how I walk, how long I can stand, my mobility, my independence, my mental wellbeing, and my ability to participate in everyday life, and my ability to be a part of my community. I've had multiple surgeries throughout my life, and I'm still having surgeries today. My most recent surgery was only a couple of months ago, and I'm due to have more surgery next week.

What should be recognized is that support needs can change over time. As we age, our bodies change, our conditions change, and the support we need can change, too. Another concern I have is that disability cannot always be reduced to a diagnosis, a code, or a checklist.

Different doctors and specialists can have different opinions, so to describe the same condition in different ways. If access to support depends too heavily on a diagnostic label or ticking boxes, there is a real risk that disabled people won't receive the support they actually need.

Support should be based on the whole person, how disability affects everyday life, and what will enable us to live independently, participate in our communities, and contribute to society, not just on what is written on a medical record. The hardest part has often not been my disability itself, it's been navigating systems where I feel like I have to keep explaining, justifying, and proving my needs every time they change.

That takes a huge emotional toll. I worry that this bill will make that even harder for many disabled people. As Māori, disability doesn't just affect one person, it affects the whole whānau. Whānau will always support each other because that is what we do. But support from whānau should be a choice, not an expectation, because services aren't there.

Disabled people should be able to rely on the services that are designed to support us, not solely on our families to fill the gaps. I know from my own journey that getting support isn't just about whether it exists, it's about whether people can actually access it when they need it. There is little point having support available on paper if people can't access it because of where they live, long waiting lists, transport barriers, or complicated processes.

When support is difficult to access, the pressure doesn't disappear, it simply shifts onto parents, partners, children, siblings, and carers. That places enormous emotional, physical, and financial pressure on whānau. Good disability support doesn't replace whānau, it strengthens them.

It allows disabled people to live with greater independence while allowing our whānau to be our whānau, not our only support system. My request to the committee is please listen to disabled people. Design a system that recognizes that our needs change over time, and that support should reflect our lives, not just a diagnosis or a checklist. Make support accessible, timely, consistent, and based on trust and dignity so disabled people can rely on it when they need it.

It's harder to read off my phone. Right. So disabled people are not just people who receive support. We contribute, too. We bring joy, and we bring love. We help shape families, communities, and futures in ways that are unseen, ignored, and undervalued, and we matter.

I want you to think about my family and your family. They have their own dreams, and they have their own goals, their own relationships, their own lives that they want to live. Would you truly be able to make that decision for yourself without feeling just a little bit sad about it?

A good disability support system should allow both things to be true. Disabled people should be able to have good lives, and the people who love and support them should be able to live good lives, too. I know that not every disabled person has a say family. Some people have been hurt by family, and some people have been hurt by others that they trusted. But everyone deserves support from people they trust and to be safe. That is why I am not asking for family or natural support to be forced on anyone.

I mean, what is that anyway? It's not really natural for anyone to help me take a shower, is it? It's not. My dad doesn't want to do it, and I certainly don't want a stranger to do it. You know, really. Do you really want a stranger staring at your butt? That is something that Lucy would say. But actually, that is what so many people have to put up with.

So if that has to happen, we should at least have someone we trust. I'm asking for disabled people to have the right to choose. For some people, it's family, and for some, it's not. Both choices deserve respect. If you can't tell already, I think I should make it very clear that this bill makes it easy for those choices to be taken away.

Ministers have said support is not intended to change right now, but this bill allows for any changes to be made in the future without even asking me. Real safety is not built on promises. It's built on protections. Disabled people should not have to rely on hope. Please protect our right to choose who supports us and recognize that family expertise has value.

Treat family members as fairly when they provide the same support that would otherwise be provided by paid support workers, and listen to us. When you think about this bill, please think about all the disabled people like me. People who want what everyone else wants, to belong, to be safe, to be respected, to be loved, and to have a say in who walks beside them in their life. My name is Lucy.

I am deaf. I have an intellectual disability. I have a rare chromosome disorder. There is actually no one in the world like me, but actually, there's no one in the world like you either. But you can speak for yourselves. I need help to speak for me. But what I think still matters.

I've worked in the field for 25-plus years working with families and children and youth. And by way of working in the field, I've worked with many families with disabilities.

So I'm coming from that angle as having worked in the field as a field worker and a psychologist. The first question asks, what changes do you want to see to the bill? I would like stronger protection for disabled people and their families written directly into legislation rather than left to future ministerial policies and regulations.

Specifically, remove wording that suggests family are primarily responsible for providing care and recognize that disability supports is a shared public responsibility. Include clear statutory rights to disability support, participation, independence, choice, and inclusion.

Establish an independent appeals and complaints process from the outset, allowing tāngata whaikaha and their families to challenge decisions that affect their support. Require meaningful engagement with disabled people, tāngata whaikaha Māori, whānau, disability support providers, and representative organizations before significant policy on funding changes are made.

Prevent significant reductions in support levels, eligibility, or service access through policy discussions alone by requiring parliamentary oversight or legislative amendment. I'm up to six now. The bill should explicitly acknowledge Te Tiriti o Waitangi and the Crown's obligations under it.

This should include requirements for genuine partnership with tāngata whaikaha Māori, their whānau, hapū, iwi, and Māori disability providers in decision making, policy development, service design, and monitoring. The bill should support shared decision-making and equitable outcomes for Māori, recognizing that Te Tiriti obligations extend beyond consultation to active partnership and accountability.

The next question asks, what is your biggest concern? My biggest concern is that the bill shifts responsibility for disability support away from government and onto families while providing fewer legal protections. In my work with children and families, I see the significant amount of unpaid care that families already provide.

If access to formal support can be restricted, reduced, or changed through policy decisions rather than legislative protections, families will be expected to carry an even greater burden. This creates uncertainty for disabled people and their families and increases the risk that essential supports will not be available when they are needed.

Next point, the consequences are likely to be significant. Children may miss opportunities for development, education, independence, and participation in their communities, while parents and caregivers may experience increased stress, burnout, financial hardship, and reduced ability to stay in employment.

And many of the families that I've worked with have struggles in those particular areas. And some of them have had to leave their jobs as a result. I am concerned the bill does not reflect adequately the Crown's obligation under Te Tiriti o Waitangi. Maori already experience inequities in access to disability supports and outcomes. If responsibility for care increasingly shifts to whanau without corresponding investment, support, and partnership, the burden is likely to fall disproportionately on Maori whanau. Rather than reducing inequities, this

risks entrenching and widening existing disparities for tangata whaikaha Maori, their whanau, their communities, their hapu, their iwi. The last question asks: what are your solution, what would your solutions be? My solution would be to build that bill around a rights-based approach, that provides certainty, accountability, and genuine partnership with disabled people, their families, whanau, hapu, and iwi. The bill should promote fairness, equity, participation, inclusion, and self-determination for all disabled peoples while recognizing the unique

status and rights of Maori under Te Tiriti o Waitangi. To give effect to these principles, the bill should require that disabled people and tangata whaikaha Maori to be actively involved in the design, delivery, and evaluation of policies and services that affect them.

It should also establish clear equity objectives, require regular public reporting on outcomes, and provide mechanisms for holding government agencies accountable when equitable outcomes are not achieved. And I've seen this happen in my time working in the field.

This approach would uphold dignity- Yeah... recognize both individual and collective wellbeing, and ensure that the needs, aspirations, and rights of all disabled people are reflected in decision-making. Whanau should never be put in a position of carrying responsibilities that properly belong to the Crown.

Furthermore, under Te Tiriti o Waitangi, the Crown has obligations to actively protect the wellbeing and equitable outcomes of tangata whaikaha Maori. This bill should strengthen these obligations, not create uncertainty about them. The test of this bill should not be whether it saves money in the short term.

The test should be whether it gives tangata whaikaha, children, young people, and their whanau the support, dignity, certainty, and opportunities they need to thrive. Kia ora. Kia ora. I rang CYFS, and two months later, this very small 10-month-old baby became my foster child. Now at the age of 35, she's a grown woman who touches the hearts and lives of all she meets.

She was given an official diagnosis of Lissencephaly syndrome, which is a rare and life-shortening condition. No one envisaged her living to this grand old age. Now, many years later, with multiple diagnosis, she lives an amazing life, embracing it to the fullest and to the fullness of her potential, with supports in place to enable her to live in her community with our family as her backup plan. Sometimes we are her only plan.

She requires 24/7 care due to her high and complex needs and intractable epilepsy. My husband and I provide some of this care alongside others. Parenting my son came through adopting a little fellow with Down syndrome. He came to us at two weeks of age and is now a 31-year-old powerlifting champ and artist. Our third child is affectionately known as our favorite biological child.

She gave herself that title. Life with two children with special needs has always been challenging. Not only is there everyday life to cope with, but add in CYFS, Ministry of Health, MOE, DSS, MSD, NASC, and all the other professionals. To say that dealing with the departments and ministries who are charged with supporting us was extremely hard, often to breaking point, would be an understatement. I have negotiated, argued, fought, even took CYFS to court in a bid to advocate for the two young people that had been given to us to raise.

Fighting, no matter the outcome, comes at a cost to family life, to relationships, to trust, and inevitably to one's health. I say fight because that is what it becomes. Advocacy is too mild of a description. Years and years of fighting takes a toll on every aspect of your life, and your body lives in a constant fight or flight mode as you await the next saga or battle that someone in some office causes you to live because of policies and legislation that are imposed without a real understanding of the implications to real people.

An Australian study, which I'm happy to provide a link, showed mothers of children with intellectual disability were 40% more likely to die of cancer, 150% more likely to die of cardiovascular disease, and nearly 200% more likely to die from misadventure than other mothers. I became one of those numbers in 2019 when I was diagnosed with stage two cancer, and a year later, stage four.

While I cannot totally apportion this to the stress I have lived under because of advocating and fighting for these two young people, I can say with certainty that the continual living in fight or flight mode would have played a huge part in that. Our family is able to articulate our needs to advocate and fight when necessary. We're resourceful, we're educated, and have held roles that have put us in good stead to manage individualized funding.

We are open, honest, and transparent. We will challenge systems, policies, and ministries if we feel that things are unjust, unfair, or just plain stupid. I feel for those who do not have these skills and who have to navigate the system, the changes over the past two years, and this proposed bill. Not to would raise some serious concerns. So now, according to this wording, I am family to all intents and purposes. However, I was one of four guardians, the state, hubby and I, and her birth mother.

Raising her was subject to state input, and I was never her parent in that sense. Fostering does not change a person's birth certificate or the responsibilities of family and state. Adoption, however, does. Our names are shown as birth parents on our son's birth certificate, and we willingly took on all the rights and responsibility of being his parents. It is hard enough to get foster parents, let alone foster parents for children with disabilities, and this act does not provide any protection for foster parents going forward.
